seoTitle: AnalogySemi Technical Support | Application Notes & Design Guides
seoDescription: Access AnalogySemi technical support resources including application notes, design guides, and FAE insights for motor drivers, gate drivers, and power management ICs.

faqs.question: How do I access AnalogySemi technical documentation?
faqs.answer: AnalogySemi technical documentation including datasheets, application notes, and reference designs is available through LiTong Electronics. Contact our technical support team to request specific documentation for AnalogySemi products. We provide comprehensive documentation packages for all products including detailed electrical specifications, application circuits, and layout guidelines.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our technical support team with your product requirements to access the latest documentation.

faqs.question: What types of technical support does AnalogySemi offer?
faqs.answer: AnalogySemi offers comprehensive technical support including product selection guidance, application circuit design review, PCB layout recommendations, and troubleshooting assistance. Our FAE team has extensive experience in motor control, power electronics, and analog design across industrial and automotive applications. Support is available through LiTong Electronics with local language support for China region customers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for personalized technical support tailored to your specific application requirements.

faqs.question: How can I get samples of AnalogySemi products?
faqs.answer: AnalogySemi product samples are available through LiTong Electronics. Contact our sales team with your project details including target application, estimated annual usage, and timeline. We provide free samples for qualified projects and offer evaluation kits for key products including motor drivers and gate drivers.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong Electronics sales team with your project information to request samples.

faqs.question: Does AnalogySemi provide reference designs?
faqs.answer: Yes, AnalogySemi provides comprehensive reference designs for key applications including motor control systems, power converters, and battery management systems. Each reference design includes complete schematics, BOM lists, PCB layout files, and software examples. These designs are fully tested and validated, providing a proven starting point for your development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our solutions section or contact our FAE team to access reference designs for your application.

faqs.question: How do I select the right AnalogySemi motor driver for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right motor driver depends on your motor type, voltage, current, and control requirements. For BLDC motors, consider the ANB8xxx series with integrated gate drivers and protection features. For stepper motors, the ANSxxxx series provides microstepping capability and smooth motion control. Key parameters to consider include: supply voltage range, peak output current, PWM frequency capability, protection features (overcurrent, thermal, undervoltage), and control interface (PWM, SPI, or analog). Our Motor Driver Selection Guide provides detailed parameter comparisons and application recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review our Motor Driver Selection Guide or contact our FAE team with your motor specifications for personalized driver recommendations.

faqs.question: What are the key features of AnalogySemi gate drivers?
faqs.answer: AnalogySemi gate drivers feature high-voltage capability up to 600V, excellent noise immunity with high CMTI (Common Mode Transient Immunity), fast switching speeds for high-frequency applications, and comprehensive protection features including undervoltage lockout (UVLO), overcurrent protection, and thermal shutdown. The ANGxxxx series offers single and dual-channel configurations with various input logic options (TTL, CMOS, differential). These drivers are ideal for motor drives, power supplies, inverters, and DC-DC converters requiring reliable high-side and low-side MOSFET/IGBT driving.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for gate driver selection based on your switching frequency, voltage, and isolation requirements.

faqs.question: How do I troubleshoot noise issues in my motor drive circuit?
faqs.answer: Troubleshooting motor drive noise requires systematic investigation. First, identify the noise source - check if it's conducted (on power lines) or radiated (EMI). For conducted noise, add input filtering with common-mode chokes and capacitors. For radiated noise, minimize loop areas in high-current paths and use shielded cables. Check grounding topology using a star ground configuration. Verify decoupling capacitors are placed close to power pins. Ensure gate drive signals are clean without ringing. Our Motor Control Design Guide and PCB Layout Best Practices articles provide detailed troubleshooting procedures.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow the systematic troubleshooting guide in our technical articles or contact our FAE team for assistance with specific noise issues.

faqs.question: What power management solutions does AnalogySemi offer?
faqs.answer: AnalogySemi offers a comprehensive range of power management ICs including DC-DC converters like the ANP34063 buck/boost controller for efficient power conversion, and low-dropout linear regulators like the ANL7805 for noise-sensitive analog circuits. Our power management portfolio covers applications from battery-powered devices to industrial power supplies. Each product is designed with features like thermal protection, current limiting, and enable control for robust system operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review our Power Management Selection Guide or contact our FAE team to discuss your power requirements.

articles.title: How to Select the Right AnalogySemi Motor Driver
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting AnalogySemi motor drivers based on motor type, voltage, current, and application requirements. Covers BLDC, stepper, and DC motor drivers.
content: Selecting the right motor driver is critical for achieving optimal motor control performance. This guide will help you navigate AnalogySemi's motor driver portfolio and choose the best device for your specific application.
## Understanding Motor Types
AnalogySemi offers motor drivers for three main motor types: Brushless DC (BLDC), Stepper, and DC Brushed motors.
**BLDC Motors** require three-phase drivers with commutation control. AnalogySemi's ANB8xxx series provides integrated solutions with both sensorless and sensored control options.
**Stepper Motors** require dual H-bridge drivers with current regulation. The ANSxxxx series offers microstepping capability up to 1/32 step for smooth motion.
**DC Brushed Motors** use simple H-bridge drivers with PWM speed control. These are the simplest and most cost-effective solutions.
## Key Selection Parameters
When selecting a motor driver, consider these critical parameters:
**Supply Voltage** must accommodate your power supply. For battery applications, ensure the driver works across the full battery voltage range.
**Output Current** rating should exceed your motor's stall current. Operating at 50-70% of rated current typically provides optimal thermal performance.
**Control Interface** affects system design. SPI offers configuration flexibility, while Step/Dir is simpler for stepper control.
## Application-Specific Recommendations
**Industrial Automation**: Use ANB8010 for BLDC motors up to 500W or ANS4208 for precision stepper control.
**Battery-Powered Devices**: Consider ANB4100 low-voltage drivers for single-cell Li-ion applications.
**Automotive**: Use AEC-Q100 qualified devices like ANB8015 for body electronics applications.
## Conclusion

AnalogySemi's motor driver portfolio offers solutions for a wide range of applications. By understanding your motor type, voltage, and current requirements, you can select the optimal device. Contact our FAE team for personalized recommendations.

faeInsights.content: The most common mistake I see in motor driver selection is focusing only on voltage and current ratings without considering thermal design. A driver rated for 5A might work at 5A, but the junction temperature could exceed safe limits without proper heatsinking. Always calculate power dissipation and verify thermal performance. For BLDC applications, another common issue is inadequate startup torque in sensorless mode - make sure to tune the alignment time and acceleration ramp for your specific load inertia. For stepper motors, microstepping above 1/16 often provides diminishing returns in smoothness while increasing control complexity.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Consider thermal design as important as electrical ratings. Tune sensorless startup parameters for load inertia. Use appropriate microstepping level for stepper applications.

customerCases.challenge: Customer was experiencing thermal shutdown in their stepper motor drivers during continuous operation. The drivers were rated for the current being used, but were overheating in the enclosed cabinet.
customerCases.solution: Replaced drivers with higher-current rated devices (ANS4216 instead of ANS4208) and improved PCB thermal design with copper pours and thermal vias. Also added forced air cooling.
customerCases.feedback: Thermal issues completely resolved. System now operates continuously without shutdown. Customer appreciated the thermal analysis guidance provided during the redesign.

faqs.question: What is the difference between integrated and external MOSFET motor drivers?
faqs.answer: Integrated MOSFET drivers like ANB8010 include power transistors in the package, offering compact size and simplified design but limited to lower power levels (typically <500W). External MOSFET drivers like ANB8200 provide gate drive signals only, allowing you to select external power transistors for higher current capability and better thermal management. Integrated solutions are best for cost-sensitive, space-constrained applications, while external solutions are preferred for high-power or specialized requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose integrated for <500W and compact designs. Choose external for >500W or special thermal requirements.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power dissipation in motor drivers?
faqs.answer: For integrated drivers, power dissipation is primarily conduction loss: P = I² × RDS(on). For example, ANB8010 with 150mΩ RDS(on) at 3A: P = 3² × 0.15 = 1.35W. For switching losses, add P_sw = 0.5 × V × I × (t_rise + t_fall) × f_sw. Calculate junction temperature: T_j = T_a + P × θ_ja. Ensure T_j stays below 125°C. For external MOSFET designs, calculate driver IC power separately from MOSFET losses.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate conduction and switching losses. Verify junction temperature is within limits. Add thermal margin for reliability.

faqs.question: When should I use sensored vs sensorless BLDC control?
faqs.answer: Use sensored control when: reliable startup torque is required, operation at very low speeds (<300 RPM) is needed, or precise position control is required. Use sensorless when: cost reduction by eliminating Hall sensors is important, wiring simplification is desired, or operation is primarily at medium to high speeds. AnalogySemi ANB8100 supports both modes - sensored for reliable startup, then transition to sensorless for running.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use sensored for low-speed or high-reliability applications. Use sensorless for cost-sensitive medium/high-speed applications.

faqs.question: What microstepping level should I use for stepper motors?
faqs.answer: Microstepping trade-offs: Full step provides maximum torque but high vibration. 1/4 to 1/8 step offers good balance of smoothness and torque for most applications. 1/16 step provides very smooth motion with minimal resonance. 1/32 step offers marginal additional smoothness but requires more precise current control and may reduce torque at high speeds. For most industrial applications, 1/8 or 1/16 step is optimal. For precision positioning, 1/32 step may be justified.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 1/8 or 1/16 step for general applications. Use 1/32 only for precision requirements. Consider torque vs smoothness trade-off.

faqs.question: How do I protect against motor stall conditions?
faqs.answer: Motor stall protection methods: 1) Current limiting - set maximum current threshold, driver limits current but maintains torque. 2) Stall detection - monitor current signature or encoder feedback, detect abnormal current draw or lack of motion. 3) Timeout protection - stop drive if position change not detected within time limit. 4) Thermal monitoring - detect overheating from stalled condition. AnalogySemi drivers include overcurrent protection, and some include stall detection features. Implement software monitoring for additional protection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement current limiting as minimum protection. Add stall detection for critical applications. Use thermal monitoring for additional safety.

articles.title: AnalogySemi Gate Driver Selection and Application Guide
articles.summary: Guide for selecting AnalogySemi gate drivers including isolated drivers, half-bridge drivers, and considerations for SiC MOSFET applications.
content: Gate drivers are critical components in power electronics, providing the interface between control circuits and power switches. This guide covers selection and application of AnalogySemi gate drivers.
## Gate Driver Types
AnalogySemi offers several gate driver categories:
**Isolated Gate Drivers** like ANG6202 provide galvanic isolation between control and power circuits, essential for high-voltage applications.
**Half-Bridge Drivers** like ANG5100 drive high-side and low-side switches in half-bridge configurations with integrated bootstrap supply.
**Low-Side Drivers** are simple, non-isolated drivers for ground-referenced switches.
## Key Selection Parameters
**Isolation Voltage** determines safety rating. For industrial equipment, 5kVrms reinforced isolation is typically required.
**CMTI (Common Mode Transient Immunity)** is critical for high-frequency switching. SiC applications require >50kV/μs.
**Drive Current** must be sufficient to charge/discharge MOSFET gate capacitance within required switching time.
## SiC MOSFET Considerations
SiC MOSFETs require special gate drive considerations: Miller clamp to prevent false turn-on, negative gate voltage for reliable turn-off, and fast overcurrent protection.
## Conclusion
Proper gate driver selection ensures reliable power switch operation. Consider isolation, CMTI, and drive current requirements for your specific application.

faeInsights.content: The most critical parameter for gate driver selection in modern power electronics is CMTI - Common Mode Transient Immunity. With SiC MOSFETs switching at 50-100kV/μs, a driver with inadequate CMTI can experience false triggering leading to shoot-through and catastrophic failure. I've debugged systems where replacing a 50kV/μs driver with a 100kV/μs driver completely eliminated mysterious failures. For SiC applications, I never recommend drivers with less than 100kV/μs CMTI. Another often overlooked factor is propagation delay matching in half-bridge configurations - delays should be matched within 20ns to prevent excessive dead-time requirements.
faeInsights.insightLogic: CMTI is critical for SiC applications - use >100kV/μs. Match propagation delays in half-bridge drivers. Use Miller clamp for SiC MOSFETs.

customerCases.challenge: Customer experienced intermittent shoot-through failures in their SiC-based converter. The gate driver was rated for the voltage but apparently couldn't handle the switching transients.
customerCases.solution: Replaced gate drivers with ANG6202 featuring 100kV/μs CMTI and added proper gate resistors to control switching speed. Implemented Miller clamp function.
customerCases.feedback: Shoot-through failures completely eliminated. System now operates reliably at full power. Customer was impressed by the CMTI explanation and its importance.

faqs.question: What is CMTI and why is it important?
faqs.answer: CMTI (Common Mode Transient Immunity) measures a gate driver's ability to maintain correct output state during rapid common-mode voltage transitions. During switching in half-bridge configurations, the high-side switch node experiences fast dv/dt transitions (up to 100kV/μs with SiC). Insufficient CMTI can cause the driver to output incorrect signals, leading to shoot-through. AnalogySemi ANG6202 offers 100kV/μs CMTI, suitable for SiC applications. Standard drivers with 50kV/μs may work for IGBTs but are marginal for SiC.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select drivers with CMTI >100kV/μs for SiC. Use >50kV/μs for IGBT applications.

faqs.question: How do I calculate required gate drive current?
faqs.answer: Gate drive current requirement: I_gate = Q_g / t_switch, where Q_g is total gate charge and t_switch is desired switching time. For example, SiC MOSFET with 100nC gate charge, target 50ns switching: I = 100nC / 50ns = 2A. Select driver with peak current rating > calculated value. Consider separate source and sink currents - higher sink current speeds up turn-off. AnalogySemi drivers range from 2A to 8A peak current.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate based on gate charge and target switching time. Select driver with 20% margin. Consider separate source/sink optimization.

faqs.question: When do I need isolated gate drivers?
faqs.answer: Isolated gate drivers are required when: driving high-side switches with floating source (voltage above ground), safety isolation is needed between control and power circuits (industrial/medical equipment), high common-mode voltages exist (>100V), or noise immunity is critical. Non-isolated drivers can be used for low-side switches in low-voltage applications. AnalogySemi ANG6202 provides 5kVrms reinforced isolation suitable for industrial and medical applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use isolated drivers for high-side, high-voltage, or safety-critical applications. Use non-isolated for simple low-side drives.

faqs.question: What is Miller clamp and when do I need it?
faqs.answer: Miller clamp prevents false turn-on caused by Miller capacitance coupling during high dv/dt switching. When the drain voltage rises rapidly, current flows through C_gd (Miller capacitance) and can raise gate voltage above threshold. Miller clamp provides low-impedance path to ground when gate is supposed to be off. Essential for SiC MOSFETs due to their high switching speed. ANG6202 integrates Miller clamp with 2A capability. For highest reliability with SiC, use Miller clamp even with negative gate voltage.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Miller clamp for all SiC applications. Recommended for high-speed IGBT applications too.

faqs.question: How do I set dead-time in half-bridge drivers?
faqs.answer: Dead-time prevents shoot-through by ensuring both high-side and low-side switches are never on simultaneously. Set dead-time to 2-3x the MOSFET turn-off time. For MOSFETs with 100ns turn-off, use 200-300ns dead-time. ANG5100 uses external resistor: R_DT (kΩ) = (t_dead - 100ns) / 0.01. For 500ns dead-time: R = 40kΩ. Too little dead-time risks shoot-through; too much dead-time increases losses and output distortion. Always verify with oscilloscope measurement.
faqs.decisionGuide: Set to 2-3x turn-off time. Calculate resistor value. Verify with oscilloscope.

articles.title: PCB Layout Best Practices for Motor and Power Applications
articles.summary: Essential PCB layout guidelines for motor drives and power converters using AnalogySemi ICs. Covers grounding, power distribution, thermal management, and EMI reduction.
content: Proper PCB layout is critical for reliable operation of motor drives and power converters. This guide covers essential layout practices for AnalogySemi-based designs.
## Grounding Strategy
Implement a star grounding topology where power ground and signal ground connect at a single point. This prevents ground loops and noise coupling.
Separate high-current power ground from sensitive analog ground. Connect them at the controller IC or at a single point near the power entry.
Use ground planes rather than traces for low impedance return paths.
## Power Distribution
Place decoupling capacitors as close as possible to IC power pins. Use multiple capacitor values (100nF, 1μF, 10μF) to cover different frequency ranges.
Minimize trace length between capacitor and IC pins to reduce inductance.
Use wide traces or copper pours for high-current paths.
## Thermal Management
Provide adequate copper area for heat dissipation. Use thermal vias to transfer heat to internal layers.
Keep heat-generating components away from temperature-sensitive circuits.
## EMI Reduction
Minimize loop areas in high-current switching paths. Keep high-frequency switching nodes away from sensitive circuits.
Use shielding and filtering for conducted and radiated EMI compliance.

faeInsights.content: I've reviewed hundreds of motor drive PCB layouts, and the most common issues are grounding-related. Designers often route high-current return paths through the same ground traces as sensitive analog circuits, creating voltage drops that couple noise into control signals. One memorable case involved a 3-phase motor drive where the Hall sensor signals were picking up switching noise because their ground return shared a trace with the motor current. Separating the grounds and using a star connection reduced noise by 20dB. Another common mistake is insufficient decoupling - placing capacitors far from ICs or using only one value. I always recommend placing 100nF ceramic within 2mm of each power pin, with bulk capacitance (10-47μF) nearby.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Use star grounding with separation of power and analog grounds. Place decoupling capacitors close to ICs. Minimize high-current loop areas.

customerCases.challenge: Customer's motor drive was experiencing erratic operation and occasional resets during high-current transients. The control signals appeared noisy on oscilloscope.
customerCases.solution: Redesigned PCB with proper star grounding, separated power and analog grounds, added more decoupling capacitors close to ICs, and optimized high-current trace routing.
customerCases.feedback: Noise issues completely resolved. System operates reliably even during rapid current transients. Customer appreciated the detailed layout review and recommendations.

faqs.question: How should I route ground connections in motor drives?
faqs.answer: Use star grounding topology: Connect all high-current power grounds (motor phases, DC bus) at a central point. Connect all sensitive analog grounds (sensors, control ICs) at a separate point. Connect these two points at a single location, typically near the controller IC or power entry. Avoid daisy-chaining ground connections. Use ground planes for low impedance. Never route motor return current through traces shared with sensitive analog circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement star grounding. Separate power and analog grounds. Single point connection between ground types.

faqs.question: What decoupling capacitors do I need and where should I place them?
faqs.answer: Use a combination of capacitor values: 100nF ceramic close to each power pin for high-frequency decoupling, 1-10μF ceramic for mid-frequency, and 47-100μF electrolytic or tantalum for bulk storage. Place 100nF capacitors within 2-3mm of IC power pins. Place bulk capacitors near power entry points. Use multiple vias to ground plane for low inductance connection. For motor drivers, add extra bulk capacitance on motor supply to handle current surges.
faqs.decisionGuide: Place 100nF close to each power pin. Add bulk capacitance at power entry. Use multiple vias to ground.

faqs.question: How do I minimize EMI in motor drive layouts?
faqs.answer: EMI reduction techniques: Minimize loop areas in high-current switching paths by placing components close together. Route high dv/dt traces (switch nodes) away from sensitive circuits. Use ground planes to provide shielding. Add RC snubbers across motor terminals to reduce ringing. Use common-mode chokes on input power lines. Keep motor cables short or use shielded cables with proper grounding. Place input filter components close to connector. Follow CISPR guidelines for creepage and clearance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Minimize switching loop areas. Route noisy traces away from sensitive circuits. Use filtering and shielding.

faqs.question: What thermal design considerations are important?
faqs.answer: Thermal design guidelines: Calculate power dissipation for all heat-generating components. Provide adequate copper area - use 2oz copper for high-current applications. Add thermal vias (0.3mm drill, 1mm pitch) under power ICs to transfer heat to internal layers. Keep hot components away from temperature-sensitive parts like electrolytic capacitors. Consider forced air cooling for dissipations >5W. Use thermal interface material between IC and heatsink. Verify thermal design with IR camera or thermocouples during testing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate dissipation. Use adequate copper area and thermal vias. Consider forced air for high power. Verify with testing.

faqs.question: How should I route gate drive signals?
faqs.answer: Gate drive routing guidelines: Keep gate drive traces short and wide to minimize inductance. Use Kelvin connection - separate gate drive return from power current path. Route gate drive traces away from high-current switching nodes to prevent coupling. Use ground plane beneath gate drive traces for shielding. For isolated drivers, maintain creepage and clearance distances per isolation requirements. Add series gate resistor close to MOSFET gate, not at driver. For high-side drives, minimize bootstrap capacitor loop area.
faqs.decisionGuide: Keep traces short and wide. Use Kelvin connection. Route away from switching nodes. Place gate resistor at MOSFET.

articles.title: Thermal Design and Management for Power Electronics
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for thermal design of motor drives and power converters using AnalogySemi ICs. Covers thermal calculations, heatsink selection, and reliability considerations.
content: Thermal management is critical for reliable operation of power electronics. This guide covers thermal design principles and practical implementation for AnalogySemi-based designs.
## Thermal Fundamentals

Heat flows from junction to ambient through a thermal resistance path. The basic equation is: T_j = T_a + P × θ_ja, where T_j is junction temperature, T_a is ambient temperature, P is power dissipation, and θ_ja is junction-to-ambient thermal resistance.
For reliable operation, keep T_j below 125°C (or manufacturer's maximum rating). Design for 80% of maximum to account for variations.
## Thermal Resistance Paths
Junction-to-case (θ_jc): Thermal resistance from die to package surface. Fixed by device packaging.
Case-to-heatsink (θ_cs): Interface resistance between package and heatsink. Depends on thermal interface material.
Heatsink-to-ambient (θ_sa): Heatsink thermal resistance. Depends on heatsink size, material, and airflow.
## Heatsink Selection
Calculate required thermal resistance: θ_sa = (T_j_max - T_a) / P - θ_jc - θ_cs. Select heatsink with lower thermal resistance than calculated.
Natural convection requires larger heatsinks than forced air. Consider airflow direction and obstruction.
## PCB Thermal Design
Use copper pours and thermal vias to spread heat. 2oz copper provides better thermal conductivity than 1oz.
Thermal vias should be 0.3mm drill with 1mm pitch, filled or plated for best conductivity.

faeInsights.content: The most common thermal design mistake I see is designing for nominal conditions rather than worst-case. A design that works at 25°C ambient may fail at 50°C ambient or at maximum load. Always design for worst-case: maximum ambient temperature, maximum load current, and minimum airflow. Another issue is ignoring the thermal interface - even the best heatsink is ineffective with poor thermal contact. I always recommend thermal interface pads or grease, and proper mounting pressure. For PCB-based thermal management, many designers underestimate the number of thermal vias needed. A single via has thermal resistance of about 100°C/W, so you need many vias (20-50) for effective heat transfer. Also, remember that electrolytic capacitors are sensitive to temperature - keeping them cool extends system lifetime significantly.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Design for worst-case conditions, not nominal. Use proper thermal interface material. Add sufficient thermal vias (20-50). Keep electrolytic capacitors cool.

customerCases.challenge: Customer's motor drive was experiencing thermal shutdown during summer operation in non-air-conditioned environments. Ambient temperatures reached 50°C.
customerCases.solution: Redesigned thermal management with larger heatsink, improved thermal interface, added thermal vias under power devices, and implemented forced air cooling with temperature-controlled fan.
customerCases.feedback: Thermal issues completely resolved. System operates reliably at 50°C ambient with margin. Fan control reduces noise during normal operation.

faqs.question: How do I calculate junction temperature for a device?
faqs.answer: Junction temperature calculation: T_j = T_a + P × θ_ja. For devices with heatsinks: T_j = T_a + P × (θ_jc + θ_cs + θ_sa). Example: Device dissipates 5W, ambient 50°C, θ_jc = 2°C/W, thermal pad θ_cs = 0.5°C/W, heatsink θ_sa = 10°C/W. T_j = 50 + 5 × (2 + 0.5 + 10) = 50 + 62.5 = 112.5°C. Ensure T_j < 125°C for reliability. For PCB-mounted devices without heatsinks, use PCB thermal resistance and copper area to estimate θ_ja.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate using thermal resistance path. Include all interface resistances. Verify T_j is within limits with margin.

faqs.question: What thermal interface material should I use?
faqs.answer: Thermal interface options: Thermal grease provides best performance (0.1-0.5°C-cm²/W) but is messy and can pump out. Thermal pads are cleaner and easier to use (0.5-3°C-cm²/W) - choose based on required thermal conductivity. Phase-change materials provide good performance and long-term stability. Gap fillers for uneven surfaces. For AnalogySemi motor drivers, thermal pads with 2-3 W/m-K conductivity are typically sufficient. Ensure proper mounting pressure per material specifications. Surface finish also matters - smooth, flat surfaces provide better contact.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use thermal grease for highest performance applications. Use pads for ease of assembly. Ensure proper mounting pressure.

faqs.question: How many thermal vias do I need?
faqs.answer: Thermal via calculation: Single unfilled via (0.3mm drill) has thermal resistance of approximately 100°C/W. To achieve 5°C/W (reasonable target), you need about 20 vias. For best results, use 30-50 vias under power pads. Via specifications: 0.3mm drill, 1mm pitch, plated through. Filled vias provide better conductivity but cost more. Place vias directly under the device exposed pad, extending slightly beyond pad edges. Connect to ground planes on multiple layers for heat spreading. Avoid solder mask over vias for better heat transfer.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 30-50 vias for typical power devices. More vias for higher power. Ensure proper connection to ground planes.

faqs.question: How do I select a heatsink?
faqs.answer: Heatsink selection process: 1) Calculate required thermal resistance: θ_sa = (T_j_max - T_a) / P - θ_jc - θ_cs. 2) Select heatsink with θ_sa 20-30% lower than calculated for margin. 3) Consider airflow: natural convection requires larger heatsinks than forced air. 4) Check physical dimensions fit in enclosure. 5) Verify mounting compatibility with device package. Example: T_j_max = 125°C, T_a = 50°C, P = 10W, θ_jc = 1°C/W, θ_cs = 0.5°C/W. θ_sa = (125-50)/10 - 1 - 0.5 = 6°C/W. Select heatsink with θ_sa < 5°C/W at required airflow.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required θ_sa. Select heatsink with 20-30% margin. Consider airflow and physical constraints.

faqs.question: What is the impact of temperature on component lifetime?
faqs.answer: Temperature significantly impacts component lifetime, following Arrhenius equation. Rule of thumb: lifetime halves for every 10°C increase. Electrolytic capacitors are most sensitive - a 20°C reduction can increase lifetime by 4x. Semiconductor devices (MOSFETs, ICs) also degrade faster at high temperature. For high-reliability designs: keep electrolytic capacitors below 85°C, power semiconductors below 125°C junction, and operate at 80% of maximum ratings. Consider using film or ceramic capacitors instead of electrolytic for high-temperature applications. Thermal cycling also causes stress - minimize temperature swings through proper thermal design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Keep components cool for long lifetime. Use 80% derating for reliability. Consider capacitor type for temperature requirements.
